four_states_of_consciousness.label: Four States of Consciousness
four_states_of_consciousness.description: Waking (jagrat), dream (svapna), deep sleep (sushupti), and the fourth (turiya). Central to the Mandukya Upanishad and resonant with Tibetan presentations of mind's nature.

passages.snippet: the entire Asian dream tradition.  The Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad, one of the shortest and most concentrated of the Upaniṣads, lays out a four-state schema. Waking (jāgrat) is the state in which awareness engages with external objects through the senses. Dreaming (svapna) is the state in which awareness engages with

through the senses. Dreaming (svapna) is the state in which awareness engages with internally generated objects without sensory input. Deep dreamless sleep (suṣupti) is the state in which awareness rests without objects of any kind, and yet — and this is the crucial claim — awareness is not absent. The Upaniṣad

passages.snippet: Brief  The Upaniṣads are a group of philosophical texts composed in the late Vedic period in ancient India, roughly between 800 and 200 BCE. They emerged from a longer tradition of Vedic ritual and hymnody but represent a turn — sometimes a sharp turn — from ritual toward direct philosophical inquiry into the

the nature of reality, the self, and consciousness. The word upaniṣad literally means "sitting down near," suggesting the intimate transmission of teaching from teacher to student. which is the ultimate truth of all phenomena. Without understanding emptiness it is difficult to cut the root of the egoic self and to find liberation from its boundaries. Module 1 — The Indian Roots  *Upaniṣadic and Early Buddhist Sources*  Opening Lecture  Before we can study lucid dreaming as it developed in Asia, we have to understand a question that the Indian philosophical tradition was asking centuries before lucid dreaming

in stages. The practitioner first aims simply to recognize, while dreaming, that the experience is a dream. This is the stage that overlaps with what Western lucid dreaming calls lucidity. From there, the practice moves into transformation (changing the dream content as a demonstration of its mental nature),

stabilized through practice. This recognized nature is called rigpa, naked awareness. Dream yoga in the Dzogchen context is not principally about producing lucid dreams. It is about recognizing rigpa within the dream state, and through that recognition extending the practice into territory that ordinary waking

begins, slowly, to be recognized. This is why Bön dream practice is inseparable from waking practice. The practitioner does not begin by trying to produce lucid dreams at night. The practitioner begins by trying to be awake during the day.  Tenzin Wangyal divides the practice into three phases. The first is

calls it transformation, hua — moves between the states without being fully in either. The practitioner who understands this does not become destabilized by lucid dreaming, by waking life, or by the transitions between them. The practitioner becomes lighter.  This is a different orientation from the Tibetan one.

MILD, WILD, WBTB techniques alongside traditional Tibetan methods (visualization of the throat chakra, Guru Yoga before sleep) - **Stabilization** — staying lucid, dream control vs. surrender - **Deepening** — dream yoga proper: recognizing emptiness, working with fear, practicing virtues in the dream state -

— dream yoga proper: recognizing emptiness, working with fear, practicing virtues in the dream state - **Integration** — bardo teachings, sleep yoga (awareness in dreamless sleep), waking-life application  Each lesson combines video, text, audio guidance, and a journaling prompt.  ---  ## Community

Module 7 — Sleep Yoga and the Practice of Natural Light  Reading Focus  Sleep yoga is not the same as dream yoga, and conflating them is one of the most common mistakes

Sleep yoga is not the same as dream yoga, and conflating them is one of the most common mistakes in popular treatments. Dream yoga concerns the dream state. Sleep yoga concerns the deep, dreamless state — the state Tibetan tradition calls the natural light, and the Upaniṣads call suṣupti. The two practices

— the state Tibetan tradition calls the natural light, and the Upaniṣads call suṣupti. The two practices reinforce each other but train different things.  In sleep yoga, the practitioner aims to rest in luminous awareness as consciousness narrows toward deep sleep, and to abide there through the night. The

What is not debated is that the practice transforms sleep from an unconscious gap into a continuation of the path. The Six Yogas are: inner heat (tummo), illusory body (gyulü), dream yoga (milam), clear light (ösel), transference (phowa), and the bardo yoga. Dream yoga is the third of the six, and its placement

Inner heat comes first because it stabilizes the subtle body — the system of channels, winds, and energetic centers that the later practices depend on. Illusory body comes second because it establishes the daytime habit of seeing all phenomena, including the practitioner's own body, as appearances without

the practitioner's own body, as appearances without independent existence. Dream yoga then takes the practitioner into the dream state, where the lessons of illusory body can be tested and deepened in conditions where the dreamlike quality of appearances is harder to deny. of the Padmasambhava narrative — and serious scholars debate it — the lineage that descends from him has preserved a remarkably consistent set of dream and bardo teachings for over a thousand years. The Bardo Thödol, known in the West as the Tibetan Book of the Dead, is the most famous text of this lineage, but

and we have to understand it before we can understand why dream practice is taken so seriously in this tradition.  The Tibetan tradition enumerates six bardos. The bardo of birth and life is the ordinary waking state, lasting from birth to the moment of dying. The bardo of dreaming is the state of dreaming

life is the ordinary waking state, lasting from birth to the moment of dying. The bardo of dreaming is the state of dreaming sleep, occurring nightly. The bardo of meditation is the state entered during deep contemplative absorption. The bardo of dying is the process of death itself, lasting from the onset of

The bardo of dying is the process of death itself, lasting from the onset of the dying process until the dissolution of consciousness from the body. Module 2 Reflection Connections  The Module 2 reflection assignment asks you to describe the bardo framework and assess what it adds to dream practice. Tenzin Wangyal does not develop the bardo framework explicitly in Part One, but the philosophical

The claim that dreams arise from karmic traces, and that practitioners can transform the traces through practice, is structurally the same claim that the bardo teachings make about transitions in the bardos of dying — that the transitions are shaped by the karmic dispositions one brings to them, and that

one brings to them, and that practice transforms the dispositions. Drawing this connection in your reflection will be stronger work than treating the bardo framework as freestanding. passages.snippet: AS PRACTICE**  *The Dream Journal*  Template, Method, and Weekly Review Framework  Why a Journal  The dream journal is not optional in this course. It is the most direct evidence

to the practitioner that this is a sustained record. The notebook does not need to be expensive or beautiful. It does need to be dedicated solely to the dream journal. Mixing dream entries with grocery lists or work notes weakens the journal's function.  Keep the journal beside the bed, with a pen kept inside

assignments.  The review should be conducted in the same notebook, in a section after the daily entries — or in a separate Reading Journal kept alongside the Dream Journal. it is one of the practice's primary instruments. Without a journal, dream content is lost within minutes of waking — research suggests roughly 90% of dream recall fades in the first ten minutes if it is not recorded. The journal arrests this fading. It also, over time, builds the recall capacity itself;

not recorded. The journal arrests this fading. remain focused and avoid becoming lost in fantasy. The external antidote is to make an offering or do devotional practices like guru yoga. LAXITY The second obstacle is laxity. you are lax in practice, you drift around, clouded and perhaps comfortable, even while attending to the object of attention. some action that should be taken, such as talking with the friend with whom you are disturbed; you can decide to take such an action.
